snippet:
Polynomial compression for fixed-fold sumset sizes
Draft
April 29, 2026
Abstract
For fixed h, let N(h,k) be the least N such that every cardinality |hA| attained by a
k-element set of integers is also attained by a k-element subset of {1,...,N}. We prove,
using Rajagopal’s fixed-h theorem on the range of possible values of |hA|, that
N(h,k)≤kCh
for every fixed h. The new ingredient is a polynomial-diameter replacement for the sparse
geometric blocks in Rajagopal’s large-value construction. The replacement is carried out
at the level of the low-degree Hilbert functions, which are the only data relevant to h-fold
sumsets when h is fixed.

1 Introduction
For a finite set A ⊂ Z and a positive integer h, write
hA = {a +···+a : a ∈ A}
1 h i
with repetitions allowed. Define
R(h,k) = {|hA| : A ⊂ Z, |A| = k}.
Let N(h,k) be the least positive integer N such that
R(h,k) = {|hA| : A ⊆ {1,...,N}, |A| = k}.
Equivalently, one may work in intervals [0,N −1] and translate at the end.
The purpose of this note is to prove the following polynomial compression bound.

Theorem 1.1. For every fixed h ≥ 1 there is a constant C such that, for all k ≥ 2,
h
N(h,k) ≤ kC h.
The proof uses Rajagopal’s theorem on the range of possible fixed-fold sumset sizes. Put

Theorem 1.2 (Rajagopal [1]). For every fixed h there is k such that, for all k > k ,

For large k, the proof is constructive and gives polynomial-diameter witnesses for all values
inRajagopal’srange. Thefinitelymanysmallervaluesofk areabsorbedattheendbyincreasing
the constant in the polynomial bound.
The lower part of the range is already supplied by Rajagopal’s filling-set construction, and
itsdiameterispolynomial. Theupperpartoftherangeiswhereachangeisneeded. Rajagopal’s
large-valueconstructionusessparsegeometricprogressions;theirlow-degreegeneratingfunctions
have the right shape, but their diameters are exponential in the relevant parameters. We replace
them by polynomial-diameter lattice gadgets with the same truncated generating functions.
Since h is fixed, a fixed-dimensional mixed-radix map then embeds the resulting lattice sets
into Z with only polynomial growth of the diameter.

Lemma 2.3 (Mixed-radix embedding). Let A ⊂ ZD have all coordinates in [0,M]. If Q > hM
and

1 D 1 2 D

0 ≤ q ≤ h, and

Thus, if D = O h (1) and M ≤ kO h (1), then ϕ(A) has polynomial diameter in k.
2

Proof. Each coordinate of a sum of at most h elements of A lies in [0,hM]. Since Q > hM,
equality of the images under ϕ is equality of the base-Q digits, and hence equality of the vector
sums.

Proof. Take

Suppose two q-fold sums are equal with q ≤ h. The first coordinate gives that the two sums
use the same number q of terms. The remaining coordinates give equality of the first h power
sums of the two multisets of indices. Newton’s identities then give equality of the elementary
symmetric functions, and hence equality of the two multisets. Therefore all q-fold sums are
distinct.
Next we construct polynomial replacements for the two sparse geometric blocks in Ra-
jagopal’s proof. We use a dissociated base.

section_title: 5 The lower part of the range

5 The lower part of the range
We quote Rajagopal’s filling-set lemma in a form that records the diameter.

7 The two-fold case
We include the elementary quadratic argument for h = 2, so that Theorem 1.1 is self-contained
in the endpoint case not covered by the large-value construction.

N(2,k) ≤ 8k2+2k+2.

one further point, giving |B+B| = 2m−1+s = K −D.

8 Proof of the main theorem
We now finish the proof of Theorem 1.1. The case h = 1 is trivial. The case h = 2 is Proposition
7.1.
Fix h ≥ 3. Let a be as in Proposition 5.3, and apply Proposition 6.5 with ε = a /2. For

exactly R(h,k) for all sufficiently large k.
For the finitely many smaller values of k, finiteness is immediate: for each attainable value
chooseonewitnessingset, translateitintothepositiveintegers, andtakethemaximumendpoint

This proves Theorem 1.1.
